sections.title: What a theory of valence must explain

paragraphs.text:
This chapter begins at the right place.
Before we chase fine structural detail, we have to ask what any serious theory of valence is actually supposed to explain.

The first question is the most basic one.
Why do atoms bind at all.
Why do two hydrogen atoms settle into H two, while two helium atoms do not produce a comparable stable molecule.

The next question concerns proportion.
Chemistry does not merely tell us that atoms combine, but that they combine in definite ratios, and sometimes in more than one ratio.
The old image of little valence knobs on the surface of an atom is too crude to cope with that richness.
It may explain why hydrogen saturates at H two rather than H three, but it does not really explain how carbon can give both carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide, or why valence changes with position in the periodic table.

Then comes geometry.
A good theory must explain not only which atoms bind, but how they sit in space.
It must account for the tetrahedral H C H angles of methane, for the wider chlorine angles in chloroform, for the linear form of carbon dioxide, and for the bent shape of water.

And that is still not enough.
Modern measurements reveal bond lengths, bond angles, and finer bond properties that lie far beyond the old habit of drawing one line for a single bond and two for a double bond.
A satisfying theory should gather all of this into one account.
It should tell us whether an atom can bind, how many partners it can hold, how they will be arranged, and what the individual bond is really like.

In the end, to describe bonding is to describe how electrons are distributed around nuclei.
Early electronic theories saw those electrons as fixed point charges arranged in neat geometrical patterns, often cubes or tetrahedra, and they used that picture to support the octet rule and its link with the periodic table.

But a static picture of charges cannot be the real answer.
Electrostatics itself says that such a distribution cannot be stably at rest.
Even the earliest ionic pictures had this weakness.
Opposite ions attract, yes, but something else must keep the structure from collapsing.
And if that difficulty already appears in ionic solids, it becomes sharper still for covalent molecules like H two, where neutral atoms somehow attract one another strongly enough to bind.

Bohr's model was the first serious attempt to make the picture dynamic.
It succeeded brilliantly for the hydrogen atom by giving discrete orbits and discrete energies, and so it explained spectroscopic lines with astonishing accuracy.
But it failed for systems with more than one electron or more than one nucleus.
The deeper problem was that it carried classical ideas too far.
At the electronic scale, a sharply defined orbit is not a literal observable track.
That is why valence theory had to pass into quantum mechanics, and especially wave mechanics.
Only there do we get a framework that can, in principle and often in practice, build a coherent theory of molecular structure and properties.

Broadly speaking, two atoms form a molecule because the total energy falls when the atoms come together.
For most purposes, the decisive quantity is the electronic energy, together with the repulsion between the nuclei.
The energies of vibration, rotation, and translation matter, especially in spectroscopy, but they are usually small compared with the main electronic terms.

This is why energy sits at the center of valence theory.
Hydrogen forms H two rather than H three because the relevant electronic energy is lower for the diatomic arrangement.
Water has its particular bond lengths and its bent angle because the energy reaches a minimum there.
So a real theory must show how energy changes as the nuclei move.
From that one can recover equilibrium structure, restoring forces, and vibrational behavior.

At this point an important distinction appears.
The electronic binding energy is not quite the same as the experimentally observed dissociation energy.
Real molecules are vibrating, rotating, and translating.
Zero point vibration matters most among these corrections, while rotational and translational terms are usually smaller.
For a diatomic molecule, the observed bond energy therefore depends slightly on temperature, even though the underlying electronic binding energy does not.

For larger molecules, another complication enters.
Breaking one bond is not always the same thing as dividing the total binding energy evenly among all the bonds.
After one bond breaks, the fragments can reorganize electronically and recover part of the energy.
Water gives a simple example.
Its total binding energy is about 9 point 49 electron volts, but the first O H bond requires about 5 point 18 electron volts to break, and the second only about 4 point 31.

There is also a thermodynamic distinction.
Experimental bond energies are commonly inferred from heats of reaction measured at ordinary temperature and pressure.
Strictly speaking, those are enthalpy changes rather than pure internal energy changes, because one must also reckon with the pressure volume term.
For the purposes of this book, the main concern remains electronic binding energy, but it is useful to know what has been folded into the experimental numbers.

equations.label: H = U + PV
equations.spoken: This relation says that enthalpy equals internal energy plus the pressure volume term.
equations.note: It explains why measured heats of reaction are not exactly the same thing as pure electronic binding energies.

paragraphs.text:
The table for H two makes the scale of these corrections vivid.
The electronic binding energy is about 458 kilojoules per mole, while the observed bond energy is about 435.
The zero point vibrational correction is therefore noticeable, whereas the rotational, translational, and pressure volume corrections are comparatively small.
The lesson is sobering.
Binding energy is only a small residue left after subtracting two much larger electronic energies.
That is why accurate first principles calculations of bonding are so demanding.
And the energies involved are electrical, not gravitational, by an overwhelming margin.

visuals.label: Some energy values for H2
visuals.caption: A compact comparison of the main energy terms for molecular hydrogen. The point is not to memorize every number, but to see that zero point vibration matters and that binding energy is a small difference between far larger totals.

paragraphs.text:
A final consequence follows from this way of thinking.
Because bond energy is a small difference between large quantities, it is exquisitely sensitive to the exact identity and arrangement of the atoms involved.
That is why the hydrogen fluoride bond is far stronger than either the hydrogen-hydrogen bond or the fluorine-fluorine bond.
Why phosphorus makes stable P four while nitrogen does not form an analogous stable tetrahedron.
And why even noble gases can form substantial bonds in compounds such as xenon difluoride.
A theory of valence succeeds only if it can explain that kind of specificity.

Energy diagrams condense a great deal of chemistry into a single picture.
Figure 1.1 begins with the simplest case, a particle held near the origin by a restoring force.
The farther the particle is displaced, the more potential energy is stored, and in this idealized case the curve is a parabola.
At any chosen position, the total energy divides between potential and kinetic energy.

visuals.label: Figure 1.1
visuals.caption: Two energy diagrams placed side by side. The first shows a particle in a parabolic well, and the second translates the same logic into the potential energy curve of a diatomic molecule.

equations.label: F = -kx
equations.spoken: This says the restoring force points back toward equilibrium and grows in proportion to the displacement.
equations.note: It is the standard linear force law behind the harmonic approximation.

equations.label: V(x) = 1/2 k x^2
equations.spoken: The potential energy rises with the square of the displacement, so the graph is a parabola.
equations.note: This is the energy form associated with a linear restoring force.

paragraphs.text:
That picture immediately shows what classical vibration means.
Where the kinetic energy falls to zero, the motion turns around.
Those turning points define the classically allowed region and therefore the amplitude of the vibration.
At the bottom of the curve the force vanishes, and because the point is a minimum, the equilibrium is stable.
Quantum mechanics changes the interpretation, but not the usefulness, of the diagram.

visuals.label: Figure 1.1
visuals.caption: The vertical slices of the curve show how total energy is shared between kinetic and potential energy. The turning points are where the kinetic part falls to zero and the motion reverses.

equations.label: E = T + V
equations.spoken: This is the conservation of energy statement for the vibrating particle. Total energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy.
equations.note: It lets the diagram show, at each position, how much of the energy is kinetic and how much is potential.

equations.label: F = -dV/dx
equations.spoken: The force is the negative slope of the potential energy curve.
equations.note: A minimum in the curve therefore corresponds to stable equilibrium.

paragraphs.text:
The second part of Figure 1.1 transfers the same logic to a diatomic molecule.
When the nuclei are very far apart, the energy approaches that of the separate atoms.
As they come together, the energy falls to a minimum at the equilibrium distance.
Push them still closer, and the energy rises again because nuclear repulsion eventually overwhelms the bonding effect.
The diagram also separates the purely electronic binding energy from the smaller observed dissociation energy, which is reduced by vibrational motion.
And unlike the classical picture, the vibrational energies now come only in discrete levels.

visuals.label: Figure 1.1
visuals.caption: The molecular panel shows why a stable bond sits at an energy minimum. It also distinguishes the deeper electronic well depth from the smaller observed dissociation energy once vibration is taken into account.

paragraphs.text:
One of the central tasks of valence theory is to predict the exact shape of such a curve.
Empirically, many molecular curves can be fitted by the Morse expression.
Near the minimum, that expression reduces to a parabola, which is why the harmonic picture works so well as a first approximation.
For polyatomic molecules the problem becomes richer, because the single curve expands into a potential energy surface depending on several bond lengths and angles.
That surface is one of the master objects in the theory of chemical reactions.

equations.label: E(R) = E_AB(R) - (E_A + E_B)
equations.spoken: This defines the molecular energy relative to the energy of the separated atoms.
equations.note: It shifts the zero of energy so that the shape of the bonding curve can be described more clearly.

equations.label: Morse curve
equations.spoken: This is the Morse expression used to fit many diatomic potential curves.
equations.note: It captures both the attractive well near equilibrium and the asymmetric rise of the curve away from that minimum.

equations.label: Near minimum expansion
equations.spoken: Near the minimum, the Morse curve becomes approximately parabolic.
equations.note: That is why the harmonic picture is such a good first approximation close to equilibrium.

paragraphs.text:
Valence theory belongs to a much larger enterprise.
Quantum mechanics does not merely ask how strongly electrons bind atoms together.
In principle, it lets us calculate the whole electronic life of a molecule, from structure and geometry to spectra, collisions, and chemical change.

That larger enterprise is what we call quantum chemistry.
Its impact on chemistry has been double.
It has given chemists a whole language of ideas, and it has also made first-principles calculation increasingly real.

Coulson insists, however, that calculation by itself is not yet understanding.
A machine may confirm that one bond is stronger than another, but the explanation still has to be chemical.
It must be expressed in terms such as nonbonding repulsion, core interaction, and the distribution of electron density.

That is why the qualitative side is not a second-rate substitute for exact calculation.
The two sides correct and support one another.
A sound physical picture should survive rigorous calculation, and a good physical idea often suggests the right approximation.

At the same time, one should not underestimate what quantum chemistry has already achieved.
For very small systems, especially hydrogen and other small molecules and ions, calculations can reach or even surpass laboratory precision.
In such cases, the theory has been tested so hard that the basic equations of quantum mechanics remain remarkably secure.

Dirac could therefore say, very early on, that the underlying laws for much of physics and essentially all of chemistry were already known.
The real obstacle was practical rather than fundamental.
Complicated molecules force us, at least for now, to seek understanding at a more qualitative level.

But what does it mean to understand a molecule at all?
We never see a molecule directly.
What we possess is a convergence of evidence from diffraction, spectroscopy, magnetic methods, and related experiments.

From that evidence there emerges a picture that is simple in outline and very powerful.
The nuclei vibrate about fairly definite equilibrium positions, but their motion is small compared with the distances between neighboring nuclei.
So, for many structural purposes, we may think of the nuclei as fixed.

The electrons are different.
They are better imagined as a charge cloud whose density changes from place to place.
If one could take a time exposure of electronic motion, the blurred photograph would be denser where the electrons spend more of their time.

That is the practical meaning of the charge density, usually written as P.
At each point, it tells us how much electronic charge is present per unit volume.
Once that idea is in hand, many otherwise scattered observations begin to fall into one pattern.

visuals.label: Figure 1.2
visuals.caption: Contour map of electron density in anthracene. The peaks mark where charge is concentrated around the carbon nuclei, and the gentler bridges show how bonding can be read from the cloud itself.

paragraphs.text:
Figure 1.2 makes this wonderfully concrete.
Its contour map for anthracene joins points where the total electron density has the same value.
The carbon nuclei stand out as strong peaks, while the hydrogen positions are much less precise because hydrogen contributes so little electron density to the scattering.

The same charge density can also be calculated from quantum mechanics.
Observed and calculated densities usually agree rather well.
More importantly, the molecule often behaves almost exactly as if its electrons were smeared into a continuous cloud of this density.

That is why the concept is so central.
If the charge density is known, classical electrostatics can already tell us a great deal about the forces inside the molecule.
Chemical binding then appears as the attraction between positive nuclei and the surrounding negative cloud.

visuals.label: Figure 1.2
visuals.caption: Contour map of electron density in anthracene. The peaks mark where charge is concentrated around the carbon nuclei, and the gentler bridges show how bonding can be read from the cloud itself.

paragraphs.text:
Figure 1.2 also shows something crucial for bonding.
Most of the density remains concentrated around each nucleus, as one would expect from atoms.
Yet there is still enough density in the bridge between neighboring peaks to hold the nuclei together against their mutual repulsion.

Once you begin to think in these terms, many measurable properties fall into place.
If one atom draws charge more strongly than another, the cloud becomes polarized.
The molecule then acquires a dipole moment, and higher electric moments can be understood in the same general way.

Nuclear quadrupole resonance gives another example.
Here the nucleus itself acts as a probe of how uneven the electric field is at its position.
Because that field depends on the surrounding electron density, the experiment becomes a very direct test of bonding ideas.

visuals.label: Figure 1.3
visuals.caption: Energy diagram for photoelectron spectroscopy. The essential point is that the supplied energy is divided between the cost of ionization and the kinetic energy carried away by the emitted electron.

paragraphs.text:
Photoelectron spectroscopy offers an even sharper kind of probe.
If radiation gives an electron enough energy to escape, the leftover energy appears as kinetic energy of the ejected electron.
Figure 1.3 lays out that bookkeeping very clearly.

visuals.label: Figure 1.4
visuals.caption: Carbon one s photoelectron spectrum of ethyl trifluoroacetate. Separate peaks appear because chemically different carbon atoms bind their inner electrons by different amounts.

paragraphs.text:
Now the interesting point is that inner-shell electrons from different atoms of the same element inside one molecule need not come out with the same kinetic energy.
Figure 1.4 shows this for four carbon atoms in one molecule.
Because fluorine and oxygen pull electron density away with different strengths, each carbon environment shifts the binding of its inner electron by a slightly different amount.

visuals.label: Figure 1.5
visuals.caption: Nitrogen one s photoelectron spectrum of a cobalt complex containing amine, nitro, and nitrate nitrogens. Different nitrogen environments produce distinct peaks, so the spectrum becomes a map of local electronic surroundings.

paragraphs.text:
So the peaks separate.
The nuclei are telling us, through spectroscopy, how the charge cloud has been distorted by the surrounding substituents.
Figure 1.5 shows the same kind of logic for nitrogen, and even the differing peak sizes carry chemical information.

This is the sort of understanding Coulson wants.
Not a gush of numbers from a computer, but a visualizable physical pattern that lets the chemistry make sense.
And with that demand in view, the book now turns back to fundamentals, beginning with quantum mechanics itself and the simplest solvable problems.
